Title: Structural determinants of reductive terpene cyclization in iridoid biosynthesis

Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Short-chain dehydrogenase/reductase-like NADPH-dependent reductase; homolog of progesterone 5β-reductase
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Committed/pivotal step forming the common iridoid scaffold
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes reductive cyclization of 8-oxogeranial to nepetalactol via an enol/enolate-type cyclization intermediate, using NADPH
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms the common intermediate for plant-derived iridoids
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Produces the common intermediate nepetalactol required for biosynthesis of plant-derived iridoid monoterpenes
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Ecological mediators in plant-insect interactions and pharmacologically valuable natural products
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: In vitro biochemical and structural analysis with NADPH/NADP+ and mechanism-inspired inhibitor geranic acid
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: X-ray crystal structures of apo and ligand-bound enzyme; site-directed mutagenesis including G150A and residue swaps; enzyme kinetics by NADPH consumption; GC-MS product analysis; comparative structural analysis with DlP5βR
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful pathway enzyme for engineering iridoid production and scaffold formation in heterologous or plant systems; structural information can guide enzyme redesign for substrate selectivity and catalytic improvement
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The paper focuses on structural determinants of catalysis and substrate selectivity rather than whole-plant metabolic phenotyping. CrISY is distinguished from canonical terpene synthases and from the close homolog P5βR by active-site features controlling reductive cyclization.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CrISY is the enzyme that converts 8-oxogeranial into nepetalactol, the common intermediate for all plant-derived iridoids. 2. Crystal structures of apo and ligand-bound CrISY revealed the substrate-binding pocket and probable substrate-binding mode. 3. Structural comparison and mutational analysis identified features that distinguish iridoid synthase from progesterone 5β-reductase and help explain substrate selectivity and unusual reductive cyclization chemistry.
